_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Low serum glucose measurement (less than 70 mg/dL)

preauth.label: Clinical Notes including evaluation findings, indications for the procedure, and planned line of treatment

claim.label: Detailed Indoor case papers with treatment details

ppd_preauth.q: Was the Clinical Notes including evaluation findings, indications for the procedure, and planned line of treatment submitted?
Was the blood glucose level report submitted ?

cpd_claim.q: Was Detailed Indoor case papers with vital (BP and Pulse) and Treatment details submitted?
Was the post treatment blood glucose submitted?
Was the Detailed Discharge Summary submitted with the date of the follow-up mentioned?

common_queries: Patient's blood glucose not documented or not less than 70 mg/dL at presentation without justification.
Clinical notes missing, incomplete, or lacking evaluation findings, indications, or planned line of treatment at pre-authorization.
Blood glucose level report not submitted at pre-authorization.
Detailed indoor case papers missing, incomplete, or lacking vital signs (BP and Pulse) and treatment details at claim submission.
Post-treatment blood glucose level not submitted at claim submission.
Discharge summary missing, incomplete, or lacking the date of follow-up at claim submission.

clinical_key_pointers: Hypoglycemia is often defined by a plasma glucose concentration below 70 mg/dL; however, signs and symptoms may not occur until plasma glucose concentrations drop below 55 mg/dL.
The symptoms of Whipple's triad (symptoms of hypoglycemia, low blood glucose, and immediate relief upon glucose correction) have been used to describe hypoglycemia since 1938.

Clinical manifestations can be classified as neuroglycopenic (e.g., behavioral changes, confusion, fatigue, seizure, coma) or neurogenic (e.g., tremor, palpitations, anxiety, hunger, diaphoresis, paresthesias).
Most cases occur in diabetic patients undergoing therapeutic intervention with meglitinides, sulfonylureas, or insulin. Drugs are the most common cause.
Potential causes include critical illness, alcohol, cortisol deficiency, insulinoma, or malnourishment.
Diagnosis involves symptoms consistent with hypoglycemia and a low serum glucose measurement (< 70 mg/dL). Initial evaluation should confirm hypoglycemia.
Other pertinent labs may include insulin, proinsulin, and C-peptide levels; low C-peptide with high insulin suggests exogenous insulin.
Severe hypoglycemia is treated with intravenous (IV) dextrose followed by glucose infusion. Conscious patients can take oral readily absorbable carbohydrates (e.g., fruit juice).

For patients unable to take oral agents, a 1-mg intramuscular (IM) injection of glucagon can be administered. Follow with a complex carbohydrate food source for sustained euglycemia once the patient is more awake.
